title: Help me use AI to filter out negative trades - Scatter plot analysis included
author: herve76
selftext:
Hey. I've been working on a crypto trading strategy and created this scatter plot showing all my trades' PnL vs time of day.

  Chart explanation: Each dot represents a trade - green dots are profitable (+0.1% to +1.4%), red dots are losses (mostly -0.1% to -0.2%). The

  yellow line shows current time. I have roughly equal numbers of winning/losing trades distributed throughout the day.

  The problem: I want to use AI/ML to identify patterns that predict which trades will be negative, so I can filter them out before execution.

  My data includes:

  \- Time of day/week

  \- Price action (OHLC, wicks, volume)

  \- CVD indicators (cumulative volume delta)

  \- Market conditions at trade entry



  Questions:

  1. What ML models work best for this type of binary classification (trade/no-trade)?

  2. Should I focus on feature engineering from price action or add external data (sentiment, funding rates, etc.)?

  3. Any experience with ensemble methods for trade filtering?

  4. How do you handle the time-series nature while avoiding overfitting?



  Currently sitting at \~37% win rate but if I could eliminate even 20-30% of the losing trades, the strategy would be much more profitable.



  Any suggestions, resources, or similar experiences would be hugely appreciated!



  Thanks in advance! 🚀

comments.author: atape_1
comments.body: One key piece of information is missing, how large is your dataset?

comments.author: jeden8l
comments.body:
Lopez de Prado work discusses it. Although some smart folks say it's shite, there's fairly large amount of research done around it. 
Check Meta Learning, Meta Labelling, Hudson&Thames research materials. 
My personal research is based on this type of idea, that some signals are profitable only under specific, hidden market state and are 'filterable'. As long as it's genuine alpha and not leveraged beta

comments.author: ShitUsername420
comments.body:
Rather than binary classification id cluster your datapoints using Kmeans (or something similar in simplicity for interpretation). After that you would overlay your profitability label and calculate the proportionality of said labels in those clusters. So for eg. you could get something like:
- Cluster 1: 60% profitable
- Cluster 2: 30% profitable
- Etc.
Then just take clusters with >50% win rate and find commonalities between points in the same cluster (easy to do with Kmeans) and just trade when those specific features come up (could be as simple as a profitable group having common time of day, like hong-kong trading hours)

In any case you’ll have to heavily experiment with feature extraction (finding best statistical features, etc.), algorithms and distance formulas.

After all of this you can try some other methods specifically for time series analysis, tho in practice this will require much more compute and more work for feature engineering. For example you can try to cluster latent vectors from a variational recurrent auto-encoders and do a similar analysis.

In general you should try simple methods before anything fancy and id stay away from classification algorithms for this type of filtering.

Keep in mind i have very limited experience with financial data, just have been working in the ml field for some time.

replies.author: herve76
replies.body: Thanks for the tips. Yes, it does seem like time of day is an important factor.

comments.author: cloonderwahre
comments.body: Make a ai to detect red points and delete them 😂 /s
